coredata.dc:title: Towards temperature-dependent linear parameter-varying models for lithium-ion batteries using novel experimental design
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Sheikh, A.M.A.
Donkers, M.C.F.
Bergveld, H.J.
coredata.dc:description: This paper proposes a comprehensive approach to identifying temperature-dependent battery models using the input–output model representation in the linear parameter-varying (LPV) framework. The proposed model structure considers a simultaneous dependence of the model parameters on the battery state-of-charge (SOC), current magnitude, current direction and temperature using a suitable set of basis functions formulated using available physical and experimental knowledge. Additionally, a temperature profile design is proposed that can be used along with a current profile design to excite the relevant temperature-dependent battery dynamics during the identification experiments. Moreover, an algorithm to combine multiple identification experiments is presented so that the computational complexity of the regression problem is of the same order as that of a single experiment. Finally, several battery models with varying model order and basis-function complexity are identified for a 2.85-Ah NMC battery, which are subsequently validated using a test dataset resembling a real drive-cycle scenario under varying temperature conditions. The corresponding root-mean-squared error (RMSE) values for the model exhibiting the best voltage simulation performance are found to be 19.31, 11.93 and 6.95 mV for the “cold”, “normal” and “hot” temperature conditions, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Temperature dependence
Experimental design
LPV models

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has received financial support from the Ministry of Economic Affairs and Climate , under the grant \u2018R&D Mobility Sectors\u2019 carried out by the Netherlands Enterprise Agency. Additionally, the authors would like to acknowledge support from Cleantron, Nieuw-Vennep, Netherlands for providing the NMC cells.

head.ce:title: Towards temperature-dependent linear parameter-varying models for lithium-ion batteries using novel experimental design

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Eindhoven University of Technology, Groene Loper 19, 5612AP, Eindhoven, Netherlands

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical Engineering, Eindhoven University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical Engineering, Eindhoven University of Technology, Groene Loper 19, 5612AP, Eindhoven, Netherlands.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NXP Semiconductors, High Tech Campus 60, 5656AG Eindhoven, Netherlands
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: NXP Semiconductors, High Tech Campus 60, 5656AG Eindhoven, Netherlands.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper proposes a comprehensive approach to identifying temperature-dependent battery models using the input–output model representation in the linear parameter-varying (LPV) framework. The proposed model structure considers a simultaneous dependence of the model parameters on the battery state-of-charge (SOC), current magnitude, current direction and temperature using a suitable set of basis functions formulated using available physical and experimental knowledge. Additionally, a temperature profile design is proposed that can be used along with a current profile design to excite the relevant temperature-dependent battery dynamics during the identification experiments. Moreover, an algorithm to combine multiple identification experiments is presented so that the computational complexity of the regression problem is of the same order as that of a single experiment. Finally, several battery models with varying model order and basis-function complexity are identified for a 2.85-Ah NMC battery, which are subsequently validated using a test dataset resembling a real drive-cycle scenario under varying temperature conditions. The corresponding root-mean-squared error (RMSE) values for the model exhibiting the best voltage simulation performance are found to be 19.31, 11.93 and 6.95 mV for the “cold”, “normal” and “hot” temperature conditions, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The research data and the software code associated with this work can be found online in the form of an open-source Python package at https://github.com/tue-battery/PyBatteryID .

ce:section.ce:para: The global electrification campaign to alleviate greenhouse gas emissions has shifted the focus of recent research towards lithium-ion batteries as the plausible building blocks of the desired energy storage systems. Such battery-powered energy storage systems (BESS) can store and provide energy from low-carbon renewable energy sources for various applications, such as microgrids and transportation systems. An essential component of a BESS is given by the battery management system (BMS), which requires sufficiently representative battery models for various control-oriented operations to ensure safe and reliable battery usage. In this regard, the commonly studied battery models can be mainly categorized into (i) the physics-based models, such as the Doyle–Fuller–Newman (DFN) model [1], which can be labelled as either white-box or light-greyish depending on the model parameterization, (ii) the data-driven black-box models, such as the Neural Networks [2], and (iii) the semi-empirical models, such as the equivalent-circuit models, which can be considered as dark-greyish; refer to [3] for a perspective on the palette of grey shades representing various kinds of model structures. Quite generally, the equivalent-circuit models are preferred in a BMS environment owing to their lower computational complexity, adequate accuracy and usability for various control-oriented operations, such as state-of-charge (SOC) and state-of-health (SOH) estimation [4,5].

The equivalent-circuit models (ECMs) incorporate electric circuit components into the model structure, namely resistors and capacitors, with the intent to approximate various electrochemical interfacial dynamics inside the battery [6]. In its primitive realization, the part of the ECM corresponding to the battery overpotentials has been represented within the linear time-invariant (LTI) framework such that the values of the resistors and the capacitors remain constant during the battery operation. Unfortunately, the LTI framework cannot sufficiently represent the nonlinear and time-varying battery dynamics even for higher model orders [7], that is, when the number of resistor–capacitor networks in the model structure is increased. Alternatively, an ECM can be represented using the linear parameter-varying (LPV) framework [8], which has been introduced in the literature as an intermediate paradigm between the LTI and the nonlinear system representations. Essentially, an LPV-ECM differs from an LTI-ECM such that the model parameters depend on the so-called scheduling variable which can be considered as a collection of suitable time-varying signals affecting the values of the model parameters during the battery operation. Notably, the model parameters may depend on the battery state-of-charge, current magnitude, current direction and/or temperature to appropriately approximate the battery behaviours. A few examples of the research works related to the LPV-ECM identification can be given as follows. Namely, (i) Fan et al. identified LPV battery models using sparse Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) considering only the state-of-charge (SOC) dependence [9], (ii) Hu et al. employed subspace methods to identify LPV battery models considering the model dependence on the battery SOC, temperature and current direction [10], and (iii) Li et al. identified LPV battery models considering SOC and its rate of change (which is proportional to the battery current) as the scheduling variable components [11].

By generalizing the ECMs to the input–output (IO) models, a comprehensive approach to battery model identification using the linear parameter-varying (LPV) framework has been presented in our recent paper [12], wherein the model parameters have been considered to depend on the battery state-of-charge (SOC), current magnitude and current direction. Arguably, the battery temperature can also significantly affect the battery behaviour over both short and long-time horizons, for instance, the battery resistance increases when the battery temperature is decreased, and vice versa. Such a behaviour can be attributed to sluggish (respectively, fast) electrochemical kinetics and mass transfer at lower (respectively, higher) battery temperatures [13]. Accordingly, the battery temperature needs to be considered during the model identification procedure to yield more representative battery models that can encompass a wider battery operating regime, which requires a nontrivial extension to the work presented in [12].

Several works have been reported in the literature that investigate performance enhancements due to the incorporation of temperature into the prescribed battery models. For instance, Roşca et al. [14] identified temperature-dependence of the battery electromotive force and the battery internal resistance using an arbitrary polynomial expansion, and subsequently analysed the performance of a second-order temperature-dependent ECM at several ambient temperatures, namely − 10 °C, 0 °C and 10 °C. Furthermore, Hussein [15] utilized a zeroth-order ECM with a current-direction-dependent resistor, wherein the lookup tables for the battery capacity, the battery electromotive force and the battery resistance had been formed at the following ambient temperatures, namely, 50 °C, 25 °C, 10 °C, 0 °C, −10 °C, −20 °C and −30 °C. Moreover, Zheng et al. [16] and Li et al. [17] studied the temperature-dependence of a first-order and a second-order ECM, respectively, by forming lookup tables using the values of the identified model components at the following ambient temperatures, namely, 25 °C, 30 °C, 35 °C, 40 °C, 45 °C and 50 °C in [16], and 5 °C, 15 °C, 25 °C, 35 °C and 45 °C in [17]. Unfortunately, the aforementioned works contain a combination of the following drawbacks, that is, (i) the proposed model structures do not simultaneously incorporate the dependence on the battery temperature along with the battery SOC, current magnitude and current direction, (ii) the data informativity related to the identification experiments involving diverse temperature conditions have not been examined during the model identification procedure, and (iii) the effects of varying temperature conditions during the battery operation on the performance of the identified models have not been investigated.

In this paper, we propose a comprehensive approach to identify temperature-dependent battery models using the linear parameter-varying (LPV) framework, which can be summarized using Fig. 1. Firstly, we consider the components of the scheduling variable p as the battery SOC, current magnitude, current direction and temperature. Secondly, we motivate a basis-function set to approximate the functional dependence of the model parameters on the p -components using suitable physical and experimental knowledge available a priori. Thirdly, we present a temperature-profile design that can be used to provide ambient temperature setpoints during the identification experiment in an attempt to excite the relevant temperature-dependent battery dynamics. Subsequently, we propose an algorithm to combine multiple identification experiments, which may be necessary to obtain a sufficiently informative identification dataset, such that the computational complexity of the regression problem remains the same as that for a single experiment. Finally, we validate the identified battery models using a test dataset resembling a real drive-cycle current profile under various temperature conditions, that is, “cold”, “normal” and “hot” temperature conditions.

This paper is organized as follows. Section 2 provides a summary regarding the LPV input–output model representation. Section 3 introduces the proposed model structure using the LPV framework. Section 4 proposes a temperature-profile design used during the identification experiment. Section 5 presents an algorithm to combine multiple identification experiments, and the optimization techniques to perform variable selection and parameter estimation. Section 6 describes the experimental setup used to perform various experiments studied in this paper. Section 7 discusses the obtained results regarding the simulation performance of the identified battery models. Section 8 concludes the work presented herein.

The linear parameter-varying framework (LPV) has been proposed in the literature as an upgrade to the linear time-invariant (LTI) framework for systems whose dynamics cannot be rendered time-invariant. Essentially, the time-varying dynamics can be represented by considering the parameters of an LTI model as functionally dependent on a so-called scheduling variable p . Several LPV model representations exist, such as the LPV state-space representation and the LPV input–output representation, where only the latter has been deemed relevant for this work; refer to [8] for an overview of the LPV framework and the corresponding model representations. In effect, the LPV input–output (IO) model representation can be considered similar to an LTI-IO representation which relates the model output to the model input and the past values of the input and the output such that the corresponding parameters depend on the variable p . Concretely, the LPV-IO representation in a single-input single-output setting can be given by
(1)

n

♢ p )
k
y

=
∑
i = 0
n
b

b
i
♢ p )
k
u
k − i
where
u : Z → R
represents the input,
y : Z → R
the output,
p : Z → P ⊆
R
n
p
the scheduling variable,
k ∈ Z
the discrete time instant,
(
a
i
♢ p )
: P → R
and
(
b

♢ p )
: P → R
the p -dependent model parameters,
n
p
the number of elements in the variable p , and
n

. Additionally, the notation

. Furthermore, a special structure for the p -dependence of the model parameters will be considered in this paper similar to [12], leading to the so-called shifted-form LPV-IO representation as can be given by

)

k − i
where the time lag for the variable p is identical to that of the corresponding output/input term; see [12] for a brief discussion on the motivation behind choosing such a representation.

Similar to [12], the proposed battery model structure considers the battery electromotive force to be known a priori which can be subtracted from the battery terminal voltage, leaving behind the total battery overpotential as the to-be-modelled quantity. More formally, the battery overpotential can be expressed using the LPV input–output representation presented in (2) as given by
(3)
y
k
≡
V

−
V

(

k

T

)
= −
∑
i = 1

(

k − i
)
y
k − i
+
∑
i = 0
n

i
(
p
k − i
)
u
k − i
where y represents the total battery overpotential, V the battery terminal voltage,
V
EMF
the battery electromotive force, s the battery SOC, T the battery temperature, u the battery current, p the scheduling variable and n the model order.

The battery electromotive force (EMF) – also referred to as the equilibrated open-circuit voltage (OCV) of the battery – can be obtained using several experiments, such as Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration Technique (GITT), extrapolation to zero current, or low-current cycling; see [18] for an extensive overview of the aforementioned techniques. In this paper, the EMF function has been considered to depend on both the battery SOC as well as the battery temperature, denoted as
V
EMF
( s , T )
. Accordingly, the GITT experiment has been performed in the discharge direction involving multiple ambient temperatures to capture the temperature-dependent behaviour of the battery EMF. Note that the discharge direction has been chosen instead of both charge and discharge directions assuming negligible hysteretic behaviour of the studied battery chemistry, that is, Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxide (NMC). 1
1 In case the battery under investigation exhibits significant hysteresis phenomenon, the techniques presented in [19] may be employed along with the methodology presented in this paper. In effect, the methodology to obtain the EMF function
V
EMF
( s , T )
can be detailed as follows.

ce:list-item.ce:para:
GITT. Firstly, the GITT experiment can be performed in the discharge direction using current pulses with amplitude
α
EMF
corresponding to the SOC resolution
Δ
SOC
EMF
followed by adaptive rest periods such that the battery is kept at rest until the variation in the battery terminal voltage
Δ V
becomes less than a prespecified limit. Once the battery reaches equilibrium, the rest period following each current pulse can be extended to involve multiple ambient temperatures, namely
T
EMF
≜
{ 0 , 20 , 40 }
∘
C, for suitable durations such that the battery temperature converges to the desired ambient temperature values
T
EMF
. Note that the battery temperature can be monitored using a temperature sensor attached to the battery surface. Additionally, the current pulses can be applied at the highest ambient temperature in the set
T
EMF
to extract the maximum possible charge from the battery for a given voltage range; see Section 3.2 for the arguments concerning the calculation of the battery capacity value using the same experiment.

Finding
∂
V
EMF
/ ∂ T
curve. Secondly, the temperature-dependent behaviour of the battery EMF can be described by the
∂
V
EMF
/ ∂ T
curve obtained as a function of the battery SOC s . Essentially, the battery EMF value can be measured at the end of each temperature step
T ∈
T
EMF
during the rest period following each current pulse. Subsequently, the
∂
V
EMF
/ ∂ T
value can be determined for the SOC values corresponding to the end of each current pulse using the slope of a straight line fitted on the corresponding voltage–temperature data assuming linear temperature-dependence for the battery EMF in the temperature range given by
T
EMF
. Accordingly, a function
∂
V
EMF
/ ∂ T
( s )
can be obtained that performs linear interpolation for the desired SOC value s using the available
∂
V
EMF
/ ∂ T
and the corresponding SOC values. Note that the experimental procedure for GITT described above can be closely related to the potentiometric method used to study the entropy variations
Δ S
inside the battery, where
Δ S ∝ ∂
V
EMF
/ ∂ T
[20].

Finding a reference EMF curve. Thirdly, a reference EMF curve corresponding to a prespecified temperature value
T
ref
in the set
T
EMF
can be determined by noting the voltage value at the end of the corresponding temperature step during the rest period following each current pulse. Accordingly, a reference EMF function can be obtained that performs linear interpolation for the desired SOC value s using the available voltage and the corresponding SOC values; the function has henceforth been denoted by
V
EMF
( s ,
T
ref
)
since it represents the
V
EMF
( s , T )
function at the temperature
T
ref
.

ce:section.ce:para:
Now, given the availability of the functions
∂
V
EMF
/ ∂ T
( s )
and
V

( s ,
T
ref
)
, the battery EMF function
V

( s , T )
can be given by
(4)

)

( s )

T −

)
where the temperature
T
ref
has been chosen as
T
ref
= 20
° C
in this work. A workaround to reduce the experimental duration can be given as follows. If the
∂
V
EMF
/ ∂ T
( s )
curve can be obtained beforehand, for instance, using the results reported in the literature, the GITT experiment prescribed above can then be performed only at a single temperature
T
ref
to obtain the function
V
EMF
( s ,
T
ref
)
.

As mentioned in the introduction of this paper, the battery voltage–current relation can be argued to depend on several variables, namely, the battery SOC, current magnitude, current direction and temperature. Such dependencies can be realized in the LPV framework by collecting the relevant variables in the so-called scheduling variable. Formally, the scheduling variable p has been defined in this paper as a four-dimensional vector
p : Z → P ⊂
R
4
as can be given by
(5)
p
k
=
s
k
|
u
k
|
δ
k
T
k
T
where s represents the battery SOC,
| u |
the current magnitude, δ the current direction, and T the temperature. While the variables
| u |
and T can be understood in a straightforward manner, the definitions of the variables s and δ need further examination. More precisely, the SOC s can be defined using the well-known first-order difference equation as given by
(6)
s
k + 1
=
s
k
+
Δ t
Q
u
k
where
Δ t
corresponds to the model sampling time, and Q represents the battery capacity which can be determined at the highest value in the desired temperature range, that is, 40 °C in this work, using the coulomb counting technique with the GITT experiment described above; see Section 3.1. Note that the available battery capacity increases as the battery temperature increases, and therefore becomes closest to the theoretical capacity at the highest investigated temperature. Practically, choosing the capacity Q in such a manner ensures the variable s does not become negative and remains in the range
s ∈
[ 0 , 1 ]
. On the other hand, the variable δ can be defined using a first-order difference equation with the sign of the input current as the forcing term as given by
(7)
δ

(

)

+
1 − ɛ

)

)

sgn
( ⋅ )
corresponds to the signum function. Additionally,
ɛ : R → E ⊆ R
represents a hybrid parameter that takes on different values depending on the current
u
k
as given by
ɛ
(
u
k
)

ɛ
0
for

[ 0 , 1 ]
can be considered as the hyperparameters that define a particular current-direction trajectory denoted by
δ

]
(refer to Section 3.2 in [12] for further explanation).

The scheduling-variable dependence introduced in the LPV model structure can be explained by selecting appropriate basis functions which specify the possible functional form of the model parameters
{
a
i
}
i = 1
n
,
{
b
i
}
i = 0
n
in (3). The idea is to approximate the true mathematical relations of the parameters
{
a
i
}
i = 1
n
,
{
b
i
}
i = 0
n
with the variable p using linear combinations of the selected basis functions as well as the possible combinations of the basis functions. Such dependence on various p -components, such as SOC, current magnitude and current direction, can be motivated using the generally-known physical and experimental insights for the lithium-ion batteries, as explained in [12], which can be summarized as follows. Firstly, the SOC dependence of the parameters can be explained using a suitable functional form that can model the asymptotic blow-up behaviour of the battery impedance as the SOC approaches zero, for instance, using the so-called fractional polynomials that include the terms with negative exponents as discussed in [12]. In this paper, the non-conventional polynomial terms with negative exponents have been replaced with the exponential function of the form
e
β
1
s
β
2
since the investigated experimental conditions involve both low-SOC and low-temperature values resulting in highly nonlinear battery behaviour, which can be better modelled using the exponential functions. Note that such choice of basis functions solely depends on the user and the availability of suitable battery insights. Accordingly, the set of basis functions related to SOC s can then be given by
G
s
≜
{ s ,
e
1
2
s
}
, where
β
1
= 0 . 5
acts as a normalization factor, and
β
2
= 1
the suitable exponent to model the aforementioned nonlinearity. Secondly, the current-magnitude dependence can be explained using the exponential terms inspired by the Butler-Volmer equation, which can lead to a more parsimonious functional form than using polynomial terms. Formally, the current-magnitude-dependence can be explained using a singleton set containing an exponential term of the form
e
γ
1
| u |
γ
2
as given by
G
| u |
≜
{
e
1
10
| u |
}
, where
γ
1
= 0 . 1
acts as a normalization factor, and
γ
2
= 0 . 5
the exponent suitable for describing the current-magnitude-related nonlinear behaviour. Thirdly, the current-direction dependence of the model parameters can be given by generating a current-direction δ trajectory using Eq. (7) with the hyperparameters
(
ɛ
0
,
ɛ
1
)
=
( 0 . 01 , 0 . 99 )
, that is,
G
δ
≜
{
δ
[ 0 . 01 , 0 . 99 ]
}
.

Now, the formulation of a temperature-dependent battery model structure requires suitable basis functions that can explain the dependence of the parameters
{
a
i
}
i = 1
n
,
{
b
i
}
i = 0
n
on the battery temperature. Accordingly, the exponential term of the form
e
T
0
T
can be chosen, which can be motivated using the Arrhenius equation that has been used in the battery literature to describe the temperature dependence of various battery behaviours; see [21,22] for relating the Arrhenius equation with the battery impedance and ageing, respectively. Concretely, the basis-function set corresponding to the temperature T can be given by
G
T
≜
{
e
T
0
T
}
, where T represents the battery temperature in Kelvin, and
T
0
the normalization factor chosen as
T
0
= 273 . 15
K.

ce:enunciation.ce:label: Remark
ce:enunciation.ce:para:
The values of the hyperparameters
{
ɛ
0
,
ɛ
1
,
T
0
}
should not exhibit sensitivity to different battery chemistries in general, and only the hyperparameters
{
β
1
,
β
2
,
γ
1
,
γ
2
}
may require rough modification in certain cases, such as a higher-current race-car application discussed in [23]. For instance, the user may increase or decrease the above-given values logarithmically by powers of 2. In case the identified models do not perform adequately, it may be more suitable to select alternative functional forms rather than performing rigorous tuning of the hyperparameter values.

ce:section.ce:para:
Following the selection of basis-function sets for the p -components, the final step can be given by the generation of a bigger set of basis functions
G
( l )
that contains not only the individual basis functions but also their combinations, where l represents the nonlinearity order that specifies the order of the desired combinations of the individual basis functions. Formally, the set
G
( l )
can be given by
(8)
G
( l )
=
{
g
i
g
j
|
g
i
∈
G
( 1 )
,
g
j
∈
G
( l − 1 )
}
for l > 1
where
G
( 1 )
=
{ 1 }
∪
G
s
∪
G
| u |
∪
G
δ
∪
G
T
. For instance, the set
G
( 3 )
for
l = 3
can be given by
(9)
G
( 3 )
=
{ 1 ,
s
− 1
, … , s ln
( s )
, … , s
e
1
20
| u |
, … ,
s
3
, … , s
e
1
20
| u |
e
273 . 15 K
T
}
Note that the redundant combinations such as
s
− 1
s
, as well as the combinations involving multiple δ -trajectories can be ignored. Finally, the model parameters
{
a
i
}
i = 1
n
,
{
b
i
}
i = 0
n
can be expanded using the set
G
( l )
as given by
(10)
a
i
( p )
=
∑
j = 1
r
a
i j
g
( j )
,
b
i
( p )
=
∑
j = 1
r
b
i j
g
( j )
where
{
a
i j
}
and
{
b
i j
}
represent the to-be-estimated free model parameters,
g
( j )
the j th element in
G
( l )
, and r the number of elements in the set
G
( l )
.

An important concern during the model identification procedure is given by the informativity of the identification dataset that directly affects the quality of the identified battery models. Essentially, a suitable experimental input design is crucial to obtain an informative identification dataset that excites all the battery dynamics expected during the model application. In this regard, a design for an input current signal has been proposed in our recent work [12], that mitigates the issues with the commonly used current signals to obtain the identification dataset, namely, the constant-current pulse discharge and the randomly selected real drive-cycle signals. Formally, the proposed design can be constructed using a rectangular pulse train (RPT) consisting of
N
RPT
pulses that can be defined as a function of time k as given by
(11)
[
( RPT )
τ ,
τ
0
π
]
k
=
− 1
for
t
( i )
≤ k <
t
( i )
+
1
2
τ
( i )
0
t
( i )
+
1
2
τ
( i )
≤ k <
t
( i )
+
1
2
τ
( i )
+
τ
0
( i )
1
t
( i )
+
1
2
τ
( i )
+
τ
0
( i )
≤ k <
t
( i )
+
τ
( i )
+
τ
0
( i )
0
t
( i )
+
τ
( i )
+
τ
0
( i )
≤ k <
t
( i )
+
τ
( i )
+ 2
τ
0
( i )
where
t
( i )
represents the starting time of i th pulse in the pulse train as given by
t
( i )
=
− π
(
τ
( 1 )
+ 2
τ
0
( 1 )
)
for
i = 1
t
( 1 )
+
∑
j = 1
i − 1
(
τ
( j )
+ 2
τ
0
( j )
)
for
i > 1
Note that
τ =
{
τ
( i )
}
i = 1
N
RPT
represents the combined length of the positive and negative phases corresponding to each pulse in the pulse train with
τ
( i )
∼ N
(
μ
τ
,
σ
τ
)
,
τ
0
=
{
τ
0
( i )
}
i = 1
N
RPT
the zero-valued region following each phase of the pulses with
τ
0
( i )
∼ N
(
μ
τ
0
,
σ
τ
0
)
, and
π ∈
[ 0 , 1 )
the shift in the pulse train given by the fraction of the first pulse period
τ
( 1 )
+ 2
τ
0
( 1 )
. Subsequently, the proposed design is considered as a stochastic signal I as can be given by
(12)
I
k
=
α
k
⋅
α
0
⋅
[
( RPT )
τ ,
τ
0
π
]
k
+
ξ
k
+
ν
k
where π representing the shift in the pulse train has been considered zero for the signal design I , that is,
π = 0
. The parameter
α
0
represents the fixed initial amplitude of the pulse train, and
α
k
the amplitude of the final signal that can be considered fixed for each pulse period, that is,
α
k
=
α
( i )
for
t
( i )
≤ k <
t
( i + 1 )
where
α
( i )
∼ U
(
α
−
,
α
+
)
. Additionally, the parameters
ξ
k
and
ν
k
represent the zero-mean random signal and the fixed DC offset, respectively, that become piecewise zero during the zero-current regions, thereby not affecting the signal during the rest period following each phase of the pulses. Formally,
ξ
k
∼ N
( 0 ,
σ
ξ
)
and
ν
k
=
ν
0
when
[
( RPT )
τ ,
τ
0
π
]
k
≠ 0
, otherwise
ξ
k
=
ν
k
= 0
. Note that the main idea behind the formulation of the proposed design I is simple, namely, the frequency and time-domain properties of the input signal should match the expected input conditions during the model application given by a real drive-cycle scenario. More precisely, the input signal should contain all the expected levels of current in both charge and discharge directions for each SOC range wherein the SOC-dependent dynamics can be rendered invariant.

In this work, the quest for temperature-dependent battery models introduces an additional layer of complexity for the input signal design, namely, all the battery dynamics corresponding to the signal I as described above need to be excited for all the temperature values as well as the rates with which the temperature can change during the model application. Notably, such requirements can only be fulfilled partially from the practical perspective but can serve as a guiding principle while designing the identification experiments. Accordingly, a temperature signal design is proposed to provide set points to an environmental chamber that can be used to vary the ambient temperature during the identification experiment. Formally, the temperature signal design T can be given using the rectangular pulse train (RPT) defined in (11) as follows
(13)
T
k
=
α
̃
0
⋅
[
( RPT )
τ
̃
,
τ
̃
0
π
̃
]
k
+
ν
̃
0
where all the parameters
τ
̃
,
τ
̃
0
,
π
̃
,
α
̃
0
and
ν
̃
0
are fixed numbers. Note that the zero-valued region has been ignored for the signal design T , that is,
τ
̃
0
= 0
. Additionally, the parameters
α
̃
0
and
ν
̃
0
can be chosen such that upper and lower levels of the pulse train coincide with the maximum and minimum of the investigated temperature range. On the other hand, the parameter
τ
̃
needs to be chosen such that the battery temperature can converge to the temperature level specified by the individual phases of the pulse train considering the thermal impedance between the heat source/sink and the battery. Another subtlety can be given by the uneven heating and cooling capabilities of the environmental chamber that may necessitate the specification of different positive and negative phase lengths for each pulse in the pulse train, that is,
τ
̃
=
τ
̃
+
+
τ
̃
−
, where
τ
̃
+
and
τ
̃
−
represent the positive and negative phase lengths, respectively.

Due to the thermal impedance between the heat sink/source and the battery, the battery temperature may not change quickly enough to encompass the entire investigated temperature range for each SOC range wherein the SOC-dependent dynamics can be rendered invariant. Unfortunately, such an issue can lead to the identification dataset not containing sufficient information regarding the temperature-dependent battery dynamics, and therefore degrading the quality of the identified battery models. A possible solution can be given by the repetition of the identification experiment with different values of the shift
π
̃
for the temperature signal T while keeping the remaining experimental conditions unchanged. Preferably, the values of
π
̃
should be equally distributed between 0 and 1 so that the dynamics corresponding to each SOC region can be emphasized uniformly. Formally, if the experiment gets performed q times, the values of the shift
π
̃
can be given by
π
̃
∈
{
q
0
,
q
0
+
1
q
, … ,
q
0
+
q − 1
q
}
corresponding to each iteration, where
0 ≤
q
0
<
1
q
. Note that a suitable choice of
q
0
may increase the SOC range spanned by the identification experiment, but can be chosen as
q
0
= 0
when no experimental insight is available. Indeed, such an approach attempts to excite all the relevant battery dynamics given by the scheduling components, namely, SOC, current magnitude, current direction and temperature, thereby allowing the model identification procedure to learn such battery dynamics.

Finally, it may be important to recognize the SOC range for each temperature level corresponding to the obtained identification dataset, possibly composed of multiple experiments, beyond which the identified battery models may become invalid. Intuitively, the upper and lower limits of the SOC range of validity for a certain temperature can be given by the maximum and minimum SOC values, respectively, in the identification dataset corresponding to that temperature. Practically, an approach to determine the SOC range of validity for a certain temperature range can be given by (i) forming a convex-hull-like shape around the points filled by the identification dataset in the temperature–SOC space, and (ii) subsequently placing the largest axis-aligned inscribed rectangle inside the convex hull restricted by the upper and lower limits of the temperature range. Fig. 2 shows the temperature–SOC space for an identification dataset comprising three experiments performed with varying values of
π
̃
∈
{
1
6
,
3
6
,
5
6
}
, wherein the black dashed line represents the convex hull of the given points. Note that the shaded region in Fig. 2 gives an example to determine the SOC range of validity for an arbitrary model application that involves the temperature range given by
[ 15 , 25 ]
∘
C
, by placing the largest axis-aligned inscribed rectangle inside the convex hull restricted by the temperature levels of 15 and 25
∘
C
.

As explained in [12], the steps following the selection of suitable basis functions and the acquisition of informative identification dataset(s) can be given by (i) variable selection, that is, the selection of suitable model terms from a set of candidate model terms generated using the basis function expansion, and (ii) parameter estimation, that is, the estimation of parameters corresponding to the selected model terms. In this regard, a linear regression problem can be formulated using the input–output representation in (2) as given by
(14)
Y = Φ θ + ϵ
where
Y ∈
R
N × 1
represents the output vector containing the output measurements
{
y
k
}
k = 1
N
,
Φ ∈
R
N × M
the regression matrix containing the regression vectors
{
ϕ
k
}
k = 1
N
corresponding to each output measurement,
θ ∈
R
M
the unknown parameter vector, and
ϵ ∈
R
N × 1
the error component due to noise and unmodelled dynamics. In addition, the regression vector
ϕ
k
: Z →
R
M
contains all the candidate model terms which can be generated using the set
G
( l )
, that is,
ϕ
k
=
f
k
⊗
g
k
where
f
k
≜
y
k − 1
⋯
y
k − n
u
k
⋯
u
k − n
g
k
≜
g
k
( 1 )
⋯
g
k
( r )
=
1
s
k
− 1
⋯
e
273 . 15
T
k
s
k
⋯
Note that ⊗ denotes the Kronecker product,
f
k
: Z →
R
2 n + 1
represents the vector containing the output and the input terms,
g
k
: Z →
R
r
the vector containing the basis functions, and
M = r
( 2 n + 1 )
. Furthermore, the parameter vector θ can be expressed in terms of the free model parameters as given by
(15)
θ =
−
a
11
⋯
−
a
1 r
−
a
21
⋯
b
n 1
⋯
b
n r
T
where
{
a
i j
}
,
{
b
i j
}
correspond to the free model parameters.

Before proceeding to variable selection and parameter estimation methods, a subtlety regarding the formulation of the regression problem given in (14) using multiple experimental datasets needs to be addressed. In this regard, one straightforward approach can be given by the concatenation of the individual regression matrices (and the output vectors) corresponding to each dataset, thereby leading to a tall skinny regression matrix Φ (and a tall output vector Y ). However, such an approach has been avoided in this work due to the reason that the size of the regression problem can increasingly become computationally expensive. Accordingly, the following approach can be adopted to obtain the final regression matrix Φ , namely, given q regression matrices
Φ
1
, … ,
Φ
q
with equal number of rows, the i th row of the final matrix Φ can be given by the i th row of the matrix
Φ
j
such that the
j = i
m o d
q
, where
m o d
represents the modulo operator. Note that such an approach results in the matrix Φ that has the same size as the individual matrices
Φ
1
, … ,
Φ
q
. Practically, the matrices
Φ
1
, … ,
Φ
q
may have different numbers of rows due to different numbers of measurements obtained during each experiment. In such a case, the approach to join the matrices
Φ
1
, … ,
Φ
q
can be better explained using Algorithm 1, which can also be visualized using an example given in Fig. 3.

Now, the variable selection refers to selecting suitable columns of the regression matrix Φ , which can be performed using the Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO) resulting in several free model parameters
a
i j
and
b
i j
in the parameter vector θ to be exactly zero. Concretely, the LASSO regression can be performed as given by
(16)
θ
ˆ
LASSO
=
arg min
θ
‖ Y − Φ θ ‖
2
+
λ
1
‖ θ ‖
1
where
θ
ˆ
LASSO
represents the LASSO estimate, and
λ
1
the hyperparameter controlling the amount of regularization via the
l
1
-norm. Subsequently, a filtered regression matrix
Φ
′
∈
R
N ×
M
′
can be formed by selecting the columns of the matrix Φ corresponding to the
M
′
< M
non-zero parameters in the LASSO estimate
θ
ˆ
LASSO
, thereby leading to a new regression problem
Y =
Φ
′
θ
. Finally, the parameter estimation step can be performed using the ridge regression method as given by
(17)
θ
ˆ
Ridge
=
arg min
θ
‖ Y −
Φ
′
θ ‖
2
+
λ
2
‖ θ ‖
2
where
θ
ˆ
Ridge
represents the final model estimate, and
λ
2
the hyperparameter controlling the amount of regularization via the
l
2
-norm. Note that the hyperparameters
λ
1
and
λ
2
can be determined using the k -fold cross-validation approach [24].

In this paper, a commercial lithium-ion NMC/graphite cylindrical battery cell with a 2.85 Ah rated capacity has been studied. The battery was kept in an Espec LU-114 environmental chamber while being connected to an Arbin battery cycler in order to set the ambient temperature as desired when the battery was either charged or discharged. Note that the battery cycler and the environmental chamber were controlled using the Mits Pro (Arbin) software, where the dynamic current and temperature profiles were applied using an external computer by establishing controller-area-network (CAN) communication between the Mits Pro software and the external computer. Additionally, the battery temperature was measured using an auxiliary temperature sensor provided with the Arbin cycler by attaching it to the battery surface. Furthermore, to obtain the battery EMF function
V
EMF
( s , T )
, the GITT experiments were performed using discharge pulses with amplitude
α
EMF
= 570
mA (0.2 C) and the SOC resolution
Δ
SOC
EMF
= 5 %
followed by adaptive rest periods such that the voltage variation
Δ V < 0 . 5
mV/h. Moreover, prior to performing the identification and test experiments, the battery was charged to 4.2 V using CCCV protocol with 285 mA (0.1 C) current with 4.2 V and 10 mA as voltage and current cutoff, respectively. Also, the identification and test experiments were stopped when the battery voltage reached a lower cutoff set at 2.5 V, where the test experiments were further trimmed according to the SOC range of validity of the identified models depending on the temperature range involved in each test experiment; see Section 7 for more details where such SOC range will be mentioned explicitly corresponding to each test experiment. Finally, the battery current, voltage and temperature measurements were recorded with the sampling time
Δ t = 1
s.

Prior to battery model identification, the battery EMF function
V
EMF
( s , T )
has been obtained using the reference EMF curve
V
EMF
( s ,
T
ref
)
and the entropic coefficient curve
∂
V
EMF
/ ∂ T
as given in (4), which can then be used to calculate the battery overpotential as in (3). Fig. 4 shows the curves
V
EMF
( s ,
T
ref
)
and
∂
V
EMF
/ ∂ T
as functions of the SOC s for the cell studied in this paper. In the following, several battery models with varying model order n and nonlinearity order l have been identified using the proposed current and temperature profile designs I and T ; see (12) and (13) in Section 4. Fig. 5 shows the relevant current and temperature profiles generated using the designs I and T , respectively, with the associated parameters as given by
(18)
I :
(
α
0
= 2 ,
μ
τ
= 64
s ,
σ
τ
= 32
s ,
μ
τ
0
= 16
s ,
σ
τ
0
= 8
s ,
σ
ξ
= 0 . 5
A ,
α
−
= 0 . 1 ,
α
+
= 1 ,
ν
0
= − 0 . 75
A
)
T :
(
π
̃
= 0 ,
τ
̃
+
= 3500
s ,
τ
̃
−
= 4500
s ,
α
̃
0
= 21 . 25 ,
ν
̃
0
= 19 . 5
° C
)
Note that the actual temperature profiles used during the model identification procedure may differ from the profile shown in Fig. 5(b) by a certain amount of the shift
π
̃
. Additionally, the identified battery models have been validated using a test input current profile resembling a real drive-cycle profile with three different sinusoidally-varying temperature conditions, namely, (i) “cold” conditions with temperature range
[ 0 , 15 ]
° C
, (ii) “normal” conditions with temperature range
[ 15 , 25 ]
° C
, and (iii) “hot” conditions with temperature range
[ 25 , 40 ]
° C
. Fig. 6 shows the current profile used as the test input and the three temperature profiles simulating the “cold”, “normal” and “hot” conditions. Furthermore, the test experiments have been trimmed such that (i) the “cold” conditions traverse the SOC range
[ 0 . 9989 , 0 . 0991 ]
, (ii) the “normal” conditions traverse the SOC range
[ 0 . 9968 , 0 . 0603 ]
, and (iii) the “hot” conditions traverse the SOC range
[ 0 . 9967 , 0 . 0631 ]
, where such SOC ranges have been determined according to the SOC range of validity given by the identification datasets that have been used to obtain the battery models discussed below. Note that such trimming of the test experiments may be necessary since the test experiment may traverse a wider SOC range than the identification dataset, thereby simulating models outside of their region of validity. Firstly, a two-dimensional grid of battery models
M
l , n
2
π
̃
has been identified using (i) the current profile shown in Fig. 5a, and (ii) the temperature profile as in Fig. 5b but with the values of shift
π
̃
∈
{
2
6
,
5
6
}
, that is, the identification experiment has been performed twice (
q = 2
) with the value of
q
0
chosen as
q
0
=
2
6
. Table 1 summarizes the comparison between the simulated and the measured voltage curves using the test dataset as described above involving the “cold”, “normal” and “hot” temperature conditions. It can be seen that the root-mean-square error (RMSE) and the mean absolute error (MAE) values for the colder temperatures tend to be higher than the hotter temperatures especially for the models with the nonlinearity order
l > 1
, which can be attributed to severe nonlinearities during low SOC values at lower temperatures. Fig. 7(a)(b) and (c) show the simulated and measured voltage curves under “cold”, “normal” and “hot” temperature conditions, respectively, for the best-performing model
M
3 , 3
2
π
̃
, whose corresponding error values have been highlighted in Table 1.

Secondly, a two-dimensional grid of battery models
M
l , n
3
π
̃
has been identified by performing the identification experiment thrice with values of
π
̃
∈
{
1
6
,
3
6
,
5
6
}
. Such an exercise can be important to understand the potential for further improvement in the model simulation performance given the proposed model structure and the utilized optimization methods when the identification dataset fills more region in the temperature–SOC space. Table 2 summarizes the comparison between the simulated and the measured voltage curves for the identified models
M
l , n
3
π
̃
. It can be seen that the models
M
l , n
3
π
̃
exhibit practically similar performance as the models
M
l , n
2
π
̃
. Effectively, such an insight enables the user to reduce the experimental time since the experiment needs to be performed only twice to obtain the models
M
l , n
2
π
̃
instead of thrice for the models
M
l , n
3
π
̃
. Fig. 7(d)(e) and (f) show the simulated and measured voltage curves under “cold”, “normal” and “hot” temperature conditions, respectively, for the best-performing model
M
3 , 3
3
π
̃
, whose corresponding error values have been highlighted in Table 2.

The utility of the proposed temperature profile design T can be further emphasized by performing the identification experiment at constant temperatures of 0 °C, 20 °C and 40 °C. Essentially, a new set of models
M
l , n
const
has been obtained using the identification dataset composed of constant-temperature experiments while keeping the rest of the identification procedure unchanged. Table 3 summarizes the comparison between the simulated and the measured voltage curves for the identified models
M
l , n
const
. It can be seen that the obtained models
M
l , n
const
perform slightly worse than the models
M
l , n
2
π
̃
and
M
l , n
3
π
̃
with the best-performing model
M
3 , 3
const
exhibiting similar performance as the models
M
3 , 3
2
π
̃
and
M
3 , 3
3
π
̃
. Additionally, the models
M
l , n
const
possess a tendency to exhibit unstable behaviour (see the italicized error values in Table 3), which can be attributed to the reduced informativity of the corresponding identification dataset. Nevertheless, the models
M
l , n
const
may prove to be practically useful when the acquisition of the identification dataset using the proposed temperature profile design T may not be possible. Fig. 7(g)(h) and (i) show the simulated and measured voltage curves under “cold”, “normal” and “hot” temperature conditions, respectively, for the best-performing model
M
3 , 3
const
, whose corresponding error values have been highlighted in Table 3. Another similar case can be given by identifying battery models using a dataset comprising experiments performed at only two constant temperatures, namely, 0°C and 40°C. In this case, the model performance degrades considerably compared to the above-mentioned models. More precisely, the identified models exhibit increasingly unstable behaviour than the models
M
l , n
const
, where the best performing model corresponds to the model order
n = 3
and the nonlinearity order
l = 2
with the RMSE and MAE values given by (28.53, 86.24, 100.67) mV and (17.79, 43.48, 38.18) mV, respectively. Such a result further emphasizes the capability of the proposed design T to excite the temperature-dependent dynamics more effectively than the constant-temperature experiments while requiring less experimental time.

Finally, the significance of incorporating temperature dependence into the model identification procedure can be quantified by identifying another set of models that do not consider temperature dependence of the model parameters. Essentially, a new set of models
M
l , n
T
̄
has been obtained by (i) performing the identification experiment at constant ambient temperature of 20 °C, (ii) discarding the temperature-dependent basis functions, that is,
G
T
= ∅
, and (iii) considering fractional polynomials as the basis functions related to the SOC since the identification dataset does not contain low temperature conditions, that is,
G
s
=
{
s
− 1
, ln
( s )
, s }
. Table 4 summarizes the comparison between the simulated and the measured voltage curves for the identified models
M
l , n
T
̄
. It can be seen that the obtained models
M
l , n
T
̄
perform adequately for the “normal” temperature conditions compared to the “cold” and “hot” temperature conditions since the temperature conditions in the identification dataset closely resemble the “normal” temperature conditions. Additionally, the models
M
l , n
T
̄
perform worse for the “cold” temperature conditions compared to the “hot” conditions, which can be attributed to the severely nonlinear variation of the battery impedance as the temperature decreases below 10 °C. Fig. 7(j)(k) and (l) show the simulated and measured voltage curves under “cold”, “normal” and “hot” temperature conditions, respectively, for the best-performing model
M
3 , 3
T
̄
, whose corresponding error values have been highlighted in Table 4.

All in all, a successful model identification attempt has been demonstrated in this paper to obtain the battery models that encompass varying state of charge, current magnitude, current direction and temperature conditions. The resulting battery models can be given using the input–output representation, where the model output can be calculated by simply adding the identified model terms. For instance, the corresponding input–output equation for the model
M
1 , 1
2
π
̃
can be given by
(19)
y
k
≈ 0 . 514
y
k − 1
+ ⋯ + 0 . 158
e
1
10
|
u
k − 1
|
y
k − 1
+ 0 . 002
e
273 . 15 K
T
k − 1
y
k − 1
−
0 . 657
u
k
+ ⋯ + 0 . 151
e
273 . 15 K
T
k
u
k
+ 0 . 638
u
k − 1
+ ⋯ − 0 . 146
e
273 . 15 K
T
k − 1
u
k − 1
It may be important to note that such an input–output representation also admits a state-space representation in the LPV framework as discussed in [12], thereby rendering the identified battery models suitable for various control-oriented algorithms deployed in real BMS applications.

In this paper, a temperature-dependent model structure has been proposed along with a novel experimental design that enables the model identification procedure to effectively learn the temperature-dependent battery dynamics. It has been shown that the identification experiment may need to be repeated using the shifted versions of the temperature setpoint profile to ensure a sufficiently informative identification dataset. Furthermore, an algorithm to combine multiple identification experiments has been presented so that the size of the resulting regression problem does not exceed the largest among the individual regression problems. It may be important to note that the work presented in this paper can be considered as an extension of the work presented in [12], wherein the identified models did not consider the dependence on the battery temperature. As such, the proposed methodology may be applied to different battery chemistries and battery cell types as long as performing the desired identification experiments remains possible. Naturally, the next step in this line of research can be given by the incorporation of ageing-related battery behaviour into the proposed modelling framework, which will be considered for future studies.

A.M.A. Sheikh: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. M.C.F. Donkers: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. H.J. Bergveld: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
The research data and the software code associated with this work can be found online in the form of an open-source Python package at https://github.com/tue-battery/PyBatteryID.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has received financial support from the Ministry of Economic Affairs and Climate , under the grant ‘R&D Mobility Sectors’ carried out by the Netherlands Enterprise Agency. Additionally, the authors would like to acknowledge support from Cleantron, Nieuw-Vennep, Netherlands for providing the NMC cells.
